question: Are there Consequential Damages on the Project?
raw_answer: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract.

question: What are the are the Consequential Damages Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract. This mutual waiver includes, without limitation: damages incurred by the Owner for additional interest, penalties, taxes, insurance, additional marketing or management costs, rental expenses, for losses of use, income, profit, financing, business and reputation to Owner or to the Project, and for loss of management or employee productivity or of the services of such persons and for lost or deferred sales, rents or use of property including the Project or portions thereof or reduced sales or rent prices or diminution in value, and damages incurred by the Contractor for principal office expenses including the compensation of personnel stationed there, for losses of financing, business and reputation, and for loss of profit except anticipated profit arising directly from the Work. This mutual waiver is applicable, without limitation, to all consequential damages due to either party's termination of the Contract as provided in the Contract Documents. Nothing contained in this Section 4.3.10 shall be deemed to preclude an award of agreed liquidated damages in accordance with the requirements of the Agreement.
raw_answer: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract.

question: Is there a mutual waiver of Consequential Damages in the Contract?
raw_answer: The Contractor and Owner waive Claims against each other for consequential, special, indirect, exemplary, or punitive damages arising out of or relating to this Contract.

question: Are there Liquidated Damages on the Project?
raw_answer: The Contractor shall pay the Owner liquidated damages, as fair compensation (and not as a penalty), in accordance with the following: $2,000 per day for each day beyond the Substantial Completion date.

question: What are the are the Liquidated Damages Terms & Conditions?
value: The Owner will suffer substantial hardship and incur substantial damages if the Work is not completed on time, or if the Contractor fails to achieve Substantial Completion on or before the scheduled Substantial Completion date set forth above, as such date may be modified under the Contract Documents. The parties agree that such damages are difficult or impossible to determine with certainty, and therefore have in good faith estimated as fair compensation (and not as a penalty) the liquidated damages set forth below. If the Contractor fails to achieve Substantial Completion on or before the scheduled date set forth above, as such date may be modified in accordance with the Contract Documents, the Contractor shall pay the Owner liquidated damages, as fair compensation (and not as a penalty), in accordance with the following: $2,000 per day for each day beyond the Substantial Completion date. The assessment of liquidated damages shall be paid per calendar day until Substantial Completion shall be achieved. Liquidated damages shall be assessed cumulatively in accordance with the Contract Documents. Contractor hereby expressly agrees to waive its right to challenge the enforceability of this provision, and agrees that the liquidated damages amounts represent a reasonable measurement of damages, and not as a penalty, that Owner will sustain in the event of late performance. These liquidated damages are in lieu of Owner’s actual direct or consequential damages or losses as a result of Contractor's failure to achieve Substantial Completion of the Work. The Owner may deduct liquidated damages from any amounts due or to become due Contractor, or the Owner may require Contractor to pay any liquidated damages which exceed amounts due or to become due to Contractor within ten (10) days after Owner’s written request. Contractor waives and Owner is not required to comply with any other notice provisions in order to assess and/or deduct liquidated damages. Owner may assess and/or deduct the amount of liquidated damages by withholding the amount of the liquidated damages from the payment due to Contractor on any pay application(s) and paying that amount to itself.
raw_answer: $2,000 per day for each day beyond the Substantial Completion date

question: Is a Payment & Performance Bond Required for this project?
raw_answer: The Owner shall have the right to require the Contractor to furnish bonds, subject to Owner’s approval, which cover Contractor’s faithful performance of the Contract and payment of obligations arising thereunder.

question: What is the Payment & Performance Bond Article #?
What are the Payment & Performance Bond Article Terms & Conditions?
value: The Owner has the right to require the Contractor to furnish bonds that cover the Contractor's faithful performance of the Contract and payment of obligations arising thereunder. These bonds must be approved by the Owner and are stipulated in the bidding requirements or specifically required in the Contract Documents on the date of execution of the Contract. Upon the Owner's request, the Contractor must purchase and maintain a Performance and Labor and Materials Bond executed by a Surety with a minimum rating of A.M. Best A, in favor of the Owner, on a standard AIA bond form, A312. This bond must be provided at the time the Contractor is awarded the Contract or within three days of execution of the Contract Documents, whichever is earlier, and must remain in full force and effect through the warranty and extended warranty periods. The bond must be in an amount equal to 100% of the Contract Price, guaranteeing the Contractor's performance in full under the Contract to the satisfaction of the Owner. The cost of the bond is limited to the premium evidenced on an invoice from the Contractor's surety agent or broker. Additionally, upon the request of any potential beneficiary of the bonds, the Contractor must promptly furnish a copy of the bonds or permit a copy to be made to the Owner and any additional obligees as required for the project.
raw_answer: The Owner has the right to require the Contractor to furnish bonds that cover the Contractor's faithful performance of the Contract and payment of obligations arising thereunder. These bonds must be approved by the Owner and are stipulated in the bidding requirements or specifically required in the Contract Documents on the date of execution of the Contract.

question: What is the IMC Design Document Review obligations Article #?
raw_answer: Article 7

question: What are the Terms & Conditions / IMC's obligations for Design Document Review?
value: The Contractor is obligated to carefully study and compare the various Drawings and other Contract Documents before starting the Work and each portion of the Work. The Contractor must take field measurements of any existing conditions related to that portion of the Work and observe any conditions at the site affecting it. The Contractor shall immediately report to the Owner and the Architect, in writing, any error, inconsistency, omission, or problem with any construction feasibility it discovers. Any necessary change shall be ordered in accordance with Article 7, subject to the requirements of the provisions of the Contract Documents. If the Contractor proceeds with work without such notice to the Architect and the Owner, having discovered such errors, inconsistencies, or problems with construction feasibility, or if by such reasonable study of the site and the Contract Documents he should have discovered such, the Contractor shall bear all costs that would have been avoided by not performing the obligations in this article.
raw_answer: The Contractor must study and compare Drawings and Contract Documents, take field measurements, observe site conditions, and report any issues in writing to the Owner and Architect before starting work.

question: What is the Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Article #?
What are the Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, & Contract Document Deviations Terms & Conditions?
value: The terms and conditions for Submittal Alternates, Substitutions, and Contract Document Deviations are as follows: If the Contractor proposes to use a material that deviates from the detailed requirements of the Contract Documents, they must inform the Architect and the Owner in writing at the time of submission for approval and request written approval for the deviation. The Contractor must provide evidence that the proposed substitution or deviation will provide a quality of result at least equal to that otherwise attainable. If the Architect or Owner finds the evidence insufficient, they may reject the substitution or deviation. If a deviation or substitution is approved that requires changes in related work, the Contractor is responsible for preparing new drawings, performing additional work, and supplying additional materials at no cost to the Owner and without delaying the project. Approval of a substitution request does not relieve the Contractor from liability for non-compliance with the Contract Documents. The Contractor is also responsible for ensuring that the work complies with approved submittals and informing the Architect in writing of any deviations at the time of submittal. The Architect must give written approval for any deviations as a minor change in the work or through a Change Order or Construction Change Directive.
raw_answer: Terms and conditions for deviations and substitutions require written approval and evidence of quality, with the Contractor responsible for related changes and compliance.

question: What are the Precon Reimbursable Terms & Conditions?
value: The Precon Reimbursable Terms & Conditions include the following: The General Conditions Amount is the amount paid to reimburse the Contractor for all General Conditions costs, not exceeding $1,470,077.00 inclusive of all applicable taxes. Labor costs include wages of construction workers directly employed by the Contractor, wages or salaries of the Contractor's supervisory and administrative personnel, and costs paid or incurred by the Contractor for taxes, insurance, contributions, assessments, and benefits required by law or collective bargaining agreements. The Cost of the Work shall be credited with all rebates, trade discounts, credits on taxes, or credits realized on cash deposits or insurance premiums. Other reimbursable costs include royalties and license fees, data processing costs, deposits lost for causes other than the Contractor's negligence, legal, mediation, and arbitration costs, expenses for relocation and temporary living allowances of personnel, and costs due to emergencies. The Contractor shall only be entitled to reimbursement for direct expenditures and reasonable costs incurred as a result of termination, and for Work actually performed, excluding indirect costs or fees for work not performed.
raw_answer: The Precon Reimbursable Terms & Conditions include the General Conditions Amount, labor costs, costs paid or incurred by the Contractor for taxes, insurance, contributions, assessments, and benefits, credits for rebates, trade discounts, and other credits, royalties and license fees, data processing costs, deposits lost for causes other than the Contractor's negligence, legal, mediation, and arbitration costs, expenses for relocation and temporary living allowances, costs due to emergencies, and reimbursement for direct expenditures and reasonable costs incurred as a result of termination.

value: If a specific bidder among those whose bids are delivered by the Contractor to the Architect is recommended to the Owner by the Contractor, is qualified to perform that portion of the Work, and has submitted a bid that conforms to the requirements of the Contract Documents without reservations or exceptions, but the Owner requires that another bid be accepted, then the Contractor may require that a Change Order be issued to adjust the Guaranteed Maximum Price by the difference between the bid of the person or entity recommended to the Owner by the Contractor and the amount of the subcontract or other agreement actually signed with the person or entity designated by the Owner. Subcontracts or other agreements shall conform to the applicable payment provisions of this Agreement and the Contract Documents generally, and shall not be awarded on the basis of cost plus a fee without the prior written consent of the Owner. The Contractor shall provide the Owner with copies of all subcontracts within thirty (30) days of execution thereof.

raw_answer: ARTICLE 4

question: What are the GMP Shared Savings Terms & Conditions?
value: To the extent that the Cost of the Work and the Contractor’s Fee is less than the Guaranteed Maximum Price, the Contractor shall be entitled to share such “savings” realized by the Owner in connection with the Project as follows: --first $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 30% to the Contractor and 70% to Owner; --second $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 25% to the Contractor and 75% to Owner; --third $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 20% to the Contractor and 80% to Owner; --fourth $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 15% to the Contractor and 85% to Owner; --fifth $100,000 of savings shall be distributed 10% to the Contractor and 90% to Owner. All remaining savings shall be to and for the benefit of the Owner. The Contractor’s share of the savings as broken down above shall be capped at $100,000.

All remaining savings shall be to and for the benefit of the Owner. The Contractor’s share of the savings as broken down above shall be capped at $100,000.

question: Must the GMP Buyout Savings be Shown on Monthly Schedule of Values?
raw_answer: Yes

question: What are the GMP Buyout Savings Definition & Terms & Conditions?
value: The GMP Buyout Savings terms and conditions are as follows: The Guaranteed Maximum Price (GMP) is the total maximum cost to be paid by the Owner for the Contractor’s full and complete performance under the Contract Documents, including all services, labor, materials, and equipment necessary to construct the Work, as well as all fees, compensation, and reimbursements to the Contractor. The GMP is set forth in Section 4.3 of the Agreement. If the Cost of the Work and the Contractor’s Fee is less than the GMP, the Contractor is entitled to share the savings realized by the Owner. The distribution of savings is tiered: the first $100,000 of savings is distributed 30% to the Contractor and 70% to the Owner; the second $100,000 is distributed 25% to the Contractor and 75% to the Owner; the third $100,000 is distributed 20% to the Contractor and 80% to the Owner; the fourth $100,000 is distributed 15% to the Contractor and 85% to the Owner; the fifth $100,000 is distributed 10% to the Contractor and 90% to the Owner. All remaining savings benefit the Owner. The Contractor’s share of the savings is capped at $100,000.
raw_answer: The GMP Buyout Savings terms and conditions involve a tiered distribution of savings between the Contractor and the Owner, with the Contractor's share capped at $100,000.

question: What are the Contingency Usage Owner Notification Requirements?
value: Contractor must submit a Contingency Use Authorization form for Owner’s review and approval, fully describing the amount and basis for the proposed use of the Contractor’s Contingency and providing documentation support. The Contractor cannot use any part of the Contingency for General Conditions costs without prior submission and Owner’s written approval. Any use of the Contingency without prior submission and approval is invalid and must be returned to the Owner. Additionally, the Contractor must provide a monthly written report describing the use of the Contingency, which will be reconciled with the Owner during the pay application review process. The Owner has discretion to reject any proposed or actual use of the Contingency that does not meet the Contract requirements.

What is the Adverse Weather Days Article #?
What are the Adverse Weather Days Terms & Conditions?
value: If adverse weather conditions are the basis for a Claim for additional time, such Claim shall be documented by data substantiating the weather conditions were abnormal for the period of time as compared with the ten-year average for the local geographical area where the Project is situated, could not have been reasonably anticipated and had an adverse effect on the critical path schedule. The Contractor assumes the risks of the normal weather patterns of the local area in which the Project is located. No claim for a time extension shall be allowed on account of a failure of the Architect to furnish interpretations, instructions or drawings or to return Shop Drawings or samples until ten (10) workdays after receipt by the Architect of a written request for such instructions, drawings, or samples and only if such delay impacts the Project’s critical path and Contractor has complied with the notice and proof provisions set forth in Article 4. Contractor shall not be allowed an extension of time unless Contractor has established to the Owner’s satisfaction that the delay claimed by the Contractor is to a portion of the Work on the critical path of the Contract Schedule, and the Project is currently behind the current Contract Schedule.
raw_answer: Adverse weather conditions must be documented as abnormal compared to the ten-year average for the area, could not have been anticipated, and affected the critical path schedule. The Contractor assumes normal weather risks. No time extension claims are allowed for delays in receiving instructions or drawings until ten workdays after a written request, and only if it impacts the critical path and notice provisions are met. Extensions are only allowed if the delay affects the critical path and the project is behind schedule.

question: How many Adverse Weather Days Included in Contract Agreement?
What is the Schedule Requirements Article #?
What are the Schedule Requirements Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contract Schedule shall be based on an orderly progression of the Work, allowing adequate time for each operation, and leading to Substantial Completion by the date(s) established in the Agreement. The Contractor must coordinate its work with the work of other third-party contractors. The Contract Schedule will be reviewed by the Owner and the Architect for compliance with the requirements of this Article and will be accepted by them only in the form of an approved Change Order. Schedules submitted or used by the Contractor that are not the current Contract Schedule accepted in an approved Change Order are for the sole use of the Contractor, at his risk, and do not relate to this Contract. Unless specifically required by law, no initial payment under this Contract shall be due until the Contract Schedule has been approved by the Owner and the Architect. If the Contractor and Owner have agreed that the Contractor should be permitted to extend the time for completion as provided in Subparagraph 4.3.7, the calendar dates in the Contract Schedule shall be adjusted accordingly to retain their same relationship to the adjusted date of Substantial Completion, and the dollar value of work to be completed as the first of each month shall be adjusted pro-rata. The Contractor shall furnish sufficient forces, plant and equipment and shall work such hours, including night shifts and lawful overtime operations as may be necessary to ensure the prosecution of the work in accordance with the Contract Schedule. If, in the opinion of the Owner, the contractor falls behind schedule, the Contractor must take such steps as may be necessary to improve his progress, and must submit his plans demonstrating the manner in which the desired rate of progress may be regained. It shall be the responsibility of the Contractor to maintain his schedule so as not to delay the progress of the Work or the scheduled work of other persons. If Contractor delays the progress of the Work, in order to regain the time lost and to maintain the Contract Schedule, it shall be the responsibility of the Contractor to increase the number of shifts, days of Work, and/or to the extent permitted by law, to institute or increase overtime operations, all without additional cost to the Owner in excess of the GMP.
raw_answer: The Contract Schedule shall be based on an orderly progression of the Work, allowing adequate time for each operation, and leading to Substantial Completion by the date(s) established in the Agreement.

question: What is the Daily Log & Manpower Article #?
What are the Daily Log & Manpower Terms and Conditions?
value: § 5.2 Labor Costs § 5.2.1 Wages of construction workers directly employed by the Contractor to perform the construction of the Work at the site or, with the Owner's approval, at off-site workshops which will be billed as a Cost of the Work in accordance with Reimbursable General Conditions & Overall Schedule per Exhibit E. § 5.2.2 Wages or salaries of the Contractor's supervisory and administrative personnel when working on the Project with the Owner's approval per Exhibit E. § 5.2.3 Wages and salaries of the Contractor's supervisory or administrative personnel engaged, at factories, workshops or on the road, in expediting the production or transportation of materials or equipment required for the Work, but only for that portion of their time required for the Work. § 5.2.4 Costs paid or incurred by the Contractor for taxes, insurance, contributions, assessments and benefits required by law or collective bargaining agreements and, for personnel not covered by such agreements, customary benefits such as sick leave, medical and health benefits, holidays, vacations and pensions, provided such costs are based on wages and salaries included in the Cost of the Work under this Article 5 and Exhibit E. § 5.2.5 The Cost of the Work shall be credited with all rebates, trade discounts, credits on taxes or credits realized on cash deposits or insurance premiums, and Contractor shall take such steps as are reasonably necessary and appropriate to obtain such discounts, rebates and credits which will be applied for the benefit of Owner.

question: What is the Schedule Delay / Time Issues Article #?
What are the Schedule Delay / Time Issues Terms & Conditions?
value: The terms and conditions regarding schedule delay and time issues are detailed in several sections of the contract. If the Owner issues a written order to proceed and approves the Contractor's claim, the Contract Sum and/or Contract Time will be adjusted by Change Order. The Contractor must proceed with the work without delay, even if the claim is approved or denied in part or in full, or if the Owner instructs the Contractor to proceed subject to a later determination. Claims for additional time must be made in writing and include an estimate of the probable effect of the delay on the progress of the Work. The Contractor is entitled to an extension of time if delayed by reasons beyond their control, such as actions or inactions of the Owner, Architect/Engineer, or other contractors, changes in the Work, or catastrophic events. However, the Contractor waives any claim for damages due to delays and agrees that the sole remedy is an extension of time. Adverse weather conditions must be documented and shown to be abnormal and impactful on the critical path schedule to justify a claim for additional time. No claim for a time extension will be allowed due to the Architect's delay in providing necessary interpretations, instructions, or drawings unless it impacts the critical path and the Contractor has complied with notice and proof provisions. The Contractor must demonstrate that the delay affects the critical path and that the Project is behind schedule to be granted an extension of time. The Contractor must maintain the Contract Schedule and take necessary steps to improve progress if they fall behind. The Contractor is responsible for coordinating their work with other contractors and maintaining the schedule to avoid delays.

question: What is the Schedule Acceleration Directed by Owner Article #?
What are the Schedule Acceleration Directed by Owner Terms & Conditions?
value: In order to expedite the completion of the Work, the Owner, in his sole discretion, may direct the Contractor to increase the number of shifts, days of work, and/or perform overtime operations or to take such other measures as he deems necessary to expedite construction. Such instruction shall be in writing and signed by the Owner. The Contractor, however, shall not be entitled to any additional costs unless the need for such direction was not attributable to the actions, omissions, delays or other fault of the Contractor. In such an event, the Owner shall pay the Contractor the additional cost as mutually agreed upon in writing. If the Contractor is behind schedule due to his own actions, omissions, delays or other fault on its part and the Owner directs the Contractor to work additional shifts, overtime, expedite deliveries, supplement its forces or purchase additional materials or equipment in order to bring his work up to schedule, all additional cost in excess of the GMP shall be borne by the Contractor.
raw_answer: Owner may direct the Contractor to expedite work through additional shifts, overtime, or other measures, with costs covered by the Owner if delays are not the Contractor's fault.

raw_answer: The term 'day' as used in the Contract Documents shall mean calendar day unless otherwise specifically defined. The term 'working day' shall mean any calendar day except Saturdays, Sundays and Legal Holidays at the place of the Project.

question: What is the Substantial Completion Article #?
What are the Substantial Completion Terms & Conditions?
value: The Substantial Completion terms and conditions are detailed in the contract documents. Substantial Completion is defined as the stage when all of the Work is sufficiently complete in accordance with the Contract Documents and all Work required to license the building is complete to allow for a licensing inspection by a licensing agency. However, Substantial Completion is not reached until all necessary licenses are obtained, Certificates of Occupancy are issued, all finishes are properly installed, all as-built drawings, Operations and Maintenance manuals, and warranties are provided to the Owner, and the Architect has issued a Certificate of Substantial Completion. The Contractor must submit a list of items to be completed or corrected and all special warranties to the Architect and Owner. The Architect will inspect the Work to determine if it is substantially complete. Warranties commence on the date of Substantial Completion unless otherwise provided. The Contractor must comply with Lender requirements regarding Substantial Completion and cooperate with the Owner to facilitate compliance. The Architect will prepare a Certificate of Substantial Completion, which will establish the date of Substantial Completion and state the responsibilities of the Owner and Contractor. Partial occupancy or use of the Work may commence if the Owner and Contractor accept the responsibilities assigned to each of them. Final Completion and Final Payment occur when the Architect finds the Work acceptable and fully performed.
raw_answer: Substantial Completion terms include completion of all work, obtaining necessary licenses, issuance of Certificates of Occupancy, installation of finishes, provision of as-built drawings, manuals, and warranties, and issuance of a Certificate of Substantial Completion by the Architect.

question: What is the Payment Terms (Progress & Final) Article #?
What are the Payment Terms Retainage %?
What is the Payment Terms for Retainage Reduction Article #?
What are the Payment Terms & Conditions for Retainage Reduction?
value: The retainage will not be reduced lower than the amount required by the Lender in the Construction Loan Agreement. Payments to the Contractor are dependent upon the Owner receiving contract payments from the Lender. The Owner shall withhold as retainage 10% of each approved progress payment through 50% completion of the Project. Once a line item reaches 50% completion, no further retainage will be held on that line item. This retainage shall be withheld until Substantial Completion. Upon Substantial Completion, the Contractor shall be paid a sum sufficient to increase the total payments to 100% of the Cost of the Work and Fees, less an amount equal to 200% of the costs for incomplete Work or Punch List Work. If the remaining balance for Work not fully completed or corrected is less than retainage stipulated in the Contract Documents, and if bonds have been furnished, the written consent of surety to payment of the balance due for that portion of the Work fully completed and accepted shall be submitted by the Contractor to the Architect prior to certification of such payment.
raw_answer: The retainage will not be reduced lower than the amount required by the Lender in the Construction Loan Agreement. Payments to the Contractor are dependent upon the Owner receiving contract payments from the Lender. The Owner shall withhold as retainage 10% of each approved progress payment through 50% completion of the Project. Once a line item reaches 50% completion, no further retainage will be held on that line item. This retainage shall be withheld until Substantial Completion. Upon Substantial Completion, the Contractor shall be paid a sum sufficient to increase the total payments to 100% of the Cost of the Work and Fees, less an amount equal to 200% of the costs for incomplete Work or Punch List Work. If the remaining balance for Work not fully completed or corrected is less than retainage stipulated in the Contract Documents, and if bonds have been furnished, the written consent of surety to payment of the balance due for that portion of the Work fully completed and accepted shall be submitted by the Contractor to the Architect prior to certification of such payment.

question: What are the Terms and Conditions for Payment of Offsite Stored Material?
value: Payments for materials and equipment stored offsite may be made if approved in advance by the Owner and stored at a location agreed upon in writing. The Contractor must comply with procedures satisfactory to the Owner to establish the Owner's title to such materials and equipment or otherwise protect the Owner's interest. This includes the costs of applicable insurance, storage, and transportation to the site. The Contractor is responsible for any loss or damage to unincorporated materials or equipment stored offsite not covered by insurance.

The Contractor must comply with procedures satisfactory to the Owner to establish the Owner's title to such materials and equipment or otherwise protect the Owner's interest. This includes the costs of applicable insurance, storage, and transportation to the site. The Contractor is responsible for any loss or damage to unincorporated materials or equipment stored offsite not covered by insurance.

question: What is the Article # for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
raw_answer: Section 9.7.1

question: What are the Terms & Conditions for IMC's Stop Work Ability for late Owner payment?
value: If an undisputed payment due to the Contractor under the Contract is delayed by more than sixty (60) days, the Contractor shall have the right to pursue its rights and remedies under the Disputes provisions of the Contract. Additionally, if the Owner does not pay the Contractor within seven (7) days after the date established in the Contract Documents the amount certified by the Architect, then the Contractor may, upon seven (7) additional days' written notice to the Owner and Architect, stop the Work until payment of the amount owing has been received.
raw_answer: Contractor may stop work if payment is delayed by more than 60 days or if payment is not received within 7 days after the established date, following a 7-day notice.

question: What is the As-Built / Record Documents Requirements Article #?
What are the As-Built / Record Documents Requirements?
value: The Contractor is required to maintain at the site one record copy of the Drawings, Specifications, Addenda, Change Orders, and other Modifications, marked currently to record field changes and selections made during construction. Additionally, the Contractor must maintain a record copy of approved Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples, and similar required submittals. These documents must be available to the Architect and the Owner during the Project and delivered to the Architect for submittal to the Owner upon completion of the Work. Recorded changes must include architectural, structural, plumbing, heating, ventilating, air conditioning, electrical changes, and changes to site Work including grades and utilities. The Contractor must also maintain a Record set of all meeting minutes and a Daily Log throughout the Project, which must be available to the Architect and the Owner and delivered to the Architect for submittal to the Owner upon completion of the Work. If required by the Lender, the Contractor must provide 'As-Built' drawings and specifications that include all modifications and changes included in the Plans and Specifications. The ALTA 'as-built' survey must show the location of the Improvements upon the Real Property, all easements, and other matters affecting the site, ensuring that the Improvements are built according to the Plans and Specifications, within property boundaries, and comply with zoning ordinances.
raw_answer: The Contractor is required to maintain at the site one record copy of the Drawings, Specifications, Addenda, Change Orders, and other Modifications, marked currently to record field changes and selections made during construction. Additionally, the Contractor must maintain a record copy of approved Shop Drawings, Product Data, Samples, and similar required submittals. These documents must be available to the Architect and the Owner during the Project and delivered to the Architect for submittal to the Owner upon completion of the Work. Recorded changes must include architectural, structural, plumbing, heating, ventilating, air conditioning, electrical changes, and changes to site Work including grades and utilities. The Contractor must also maintain a Record set of all meeting minutes and a Daily Log throughout the Project, which must be available to the Architect and the Owner and delivered to the Architect for submittal to the Owner upon completion of the Work. If required by the Lender, the Contractor must provide 'As-Built' drawings and specifications that include all modifications and changes included in the Plans and Specifications. The ALTA 'as-built' survey must show the location of the Improvements upon the Real Property, all easements, and other matters affecting the site, ensuring that the Improvements are built according to the Plans and Specifications, within property boundaries, and comply with zoning ordinances.

question: What is the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Article #?
What are the Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Terms & Conditions?
value: If the Contractor encounters conditions at the site that are (1) subsurface or otherwise concealed physical conditions that differ materially from those indicated in the Contract Documents or (2) unknown physical conditions of an unusual nature, that differ materially from those ordinarily found to exist and generally recognized as inherent in construction activities of the character provided for in the Contract Documents, the Contractor shall promptly provide notice to the Owner and the Architect before conditions are disturbed and in no event later than twenty-one (21) days after first observance of the conditions. The Architect will promptly investigate such conditions and, if the Architect determines that they differ materially and cause an increase or decrease in the Contractor’s cost of, or time required for, performance of any part of the Work, will recommend an equitable adjustment in the Contract Sum or Contract Time, or both. If the Architect determines that the conditions at the site are not materially different from those indicated in the Contract Documents and that no change in the terms of the Contract is justified, the Architect shall promptly notify the Owner and Contractor in writing, stating the reasons. If either party disputes the Architect’s determination or recommendation, that party may proceed as provided in the Disputes provisions.
raw_answer: If the Contractor encounters conditions at the site that are (1) subsurface or otherwise concealed physical conditions that differ materially from those indicated in the Contract Documents or (2) unknown physical conditions of an unusual nature, that differ materially from those ordinarily found to exist and generally recognized as inherent in construction activities of the character provided for in the Contract Documents, the Contractor shall promptly provide notice to the Owner and the Architect before conditions are disturbed and in no event later than twenty-one (21) days after first observance of the conditions.

question: How much Concealed / Unknown / Hazardous Conditions Notice to Owner Requirements (Within What # of Days)?
raw_answer: twenty-one (21) days

question: What is the Cyber Indemnification / Hacking Article #?
What are the Cyber Indemnification / Hacking Article Terms & Conditions?
value: The Contractor shall defend, indemnify, and hold harmless the Owner, Additional Insureds, and Owner Parties from any claims, damages, losses, and expenses arising out of the performance of the Work, including those caused by the Contractor's negligence or willful misconduct. This obligation includes attorneys' fees and extends to claims by employees of the Contractor or its subcontractors. The Contractor's indemnification obligations are not limited by insurance policy limits and survive the completion or termination of the Contract. The Owner shall indemnify the Contractor for claims arising from hazardous materials not rendered harmless, provided the Contractor did not bring these materials to the site. The Contractor must also maintain safety precautions and comply with applicable laws to protect persons and property during the performance of the Contract.
